blurb: An indestructible little mounding shrub smothered in buttercup-yellow flowers from June to frost — cold-hardy, drought-tough, and one of the longest-blooming natives for sun.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Yellow flowers all summer into fall; fine blue-green pinnate foliage; tan seed clusters in winter

care.hardiness: Extremely cold-hardy (USDA zones 3–7), so Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters and high-altitude sun are no challenge for this circumboreal native.[1]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall into well-drained soil; widely sold as container stock and easy to establish. Potted plants are less root-hardy, so water them and shelter over winter.[1]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade; flowering is heaviest and the habit densest in full Front Range sun.[1]
care.soil: Adaptable to average, well-drained soils including lean and alkaline ground; tolerates clay if drainage is decent.[1]
care.water: Prefers evenly moist soil when young but is strongly drought tolerant once established — modest supplemental water keeps it blooming through our hot, dry summers.[1]
care.spacing: A compact mound 2–4 ft tall and 3–5 ft wide; space about 3 ft apart for a low hedge or massing.[1]
care.propagation: Propagates easily from softwood or semi-hardwood cuttings; species plants also grow from seed, and older clumps can be divided.[1]
care.bloom: Buttercup-yellow (sometimes white or orange in cultivars) saucer-shaped flowers about 1.5 in across bloom heavily from June to September — one of the longest bloom seasons of any hardy shrub.[1]
care.maintenance: Low maintenance; prune as needed in late winter and shear or cut back hard every few years to keep it from getting woody and open.[1]
care.troubles: Few problems in our dry climate; mildew, fungal leaf spots, and spider mites can appear in humid or stressed conditions.[1]

references.name: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Potentilla fruticosa (Dasiphora fruticosa)

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental shrub, not a food plant; it has a documented traditional leaf tea but no edible food use.[1]
edible.caution: Shrubby cinquefoil isn't eaten as food. Its dried leaves were sometimes brewed into a folk 'tea' (it's also called widdy or kuril tea), but that is a traditional infusion, not a documented food, and isn't a safety-vetted edible use here. No part is notably poisonous.[1]
